explicit_text: The Fall of Saigon on April 30, 1975, was a pivotal moment in Vietnam's history when the North Communist Vietnam Party took control. It forced millions, including my mother and uncle, to flee the country by boat in search of safety and a better life. This event marked the beginning of a difficult journey for many Vietnamese refugees.
implicit_text: The capture of the South Vietnamese capital on April 30, 1975, was a pivotal moment in the country's history when the communist forces from the North took control. It forced millions, including my mother and uncle, to flee by boat in search of safety and a better life. This event marked the beginning of a difficult journey for many refugees from that region.

explicit_text: Vietnam is where my family’s story begins, especially during the turbulent times around 1975. After the Fall of Saigon, my mother and uncle had to leave Vietnam in 1981 to escape the hardships and political changes. Though they were separated from the rest of our family, Vietnam remains the place of our roots and the starting point of their courageous journey.

explicit_text: My mother and uncle were among the Vietnamese boat people who fled Vietnam by sea after the Fall of Saigon. Their journey was perilous, lasting about a week with scarce food and water, and the constant fear of pirates like the Thai pirates that many others encountered. Their survival and eventual rescue symbolize the resilience of all Vietnamese boat people during that era.

explicit_text: After escaping Vietnam by boat, my mother and uncle arrived at the Malaysian refugee camp called Pulau Bidong. They stayed there for about five and a half months, sharing the space with many other refugees while waiting for their next steps. Pulau Bidong was a crucial stop in their journey toward resettlement.

explicit_text: Following Pulau Bidong, my mother and uncle were transferred to another refugee camp in the Philippines called Bataan. They stayed there for another five and a half months while their paperwork was processed for entry into the United States. Bataan was an important transitional place during their refugee journey.

explicit_text: The United States became the new home for my mother and uncle after they were accepted as refugees in 1982. They arrived in Seattle, Washington, sponsored by a couple named Mr. and Mrs. Cotton. The U.S. offered them opportunities despite language barriers, and it’s because of their sacrifices here that I have been able to pursue my dreams, including my Taekwondo career.

explicit_text: After some time in Washington, my mother and uncle relocated to Minnesota because of the Vietnamese community there and friends they knew from back in Vietnam. Minnesota became our family’s new home where my mother continued to work hard to support us. It’s where I grew up and was able to pursue my passion for Taekwondo.

explicit_text: Taekwondo has been a passion of mine, and thanks to the sacrifices my mother made, I was able to pursue it seriously here in the United States. In the summer of 2014, I made it onto the U.S. National Taekwondo Team, which was a proud moment for me and my family. Taekwondo represents not only my personal achievement but also the opportunities afforded to me by my mother’s journey.
